summary: Kenneth Suna explains covered call ETFs, highlighting that they underperform underlying stocks during rallies (Nvidia +76% vs YieldMax +22%) and carry high fees. While they provide passive income through dividends, he suggests alternatives: selling your own covered calls if you own 100 shares, owning both individual shares and the ETF to preserve cost basis and avoid taxes, or buying the ETF purely for passive income if uninterested in stock ownership.
takeaways: Covered call ETFs will underperform underlying stocks during strong rallies—example: Nvidia stock +76% YTD vs YieldMax covered call ETF +22% YTD
Covered call ETFs carry very high fees due to active management
Dividends from covered call ETFs are variable monthly based on stock volatility—Tesla dividends ranged from $2.18 to $0.80 as volatility decreased
To sell your own covered calls, you must first own 100 shares outright
Owning both individual shares (low cost basis) and the covered call ETF preserves tax efficiency and cost basis if shares get called away
If shares are called away from an ETF, you miss upside participation in further rallies

strategy_candidates.change: Establish a parallel covered call strategy using individual shares held at low cost basis alongside covered call ETF income to diversify volatility capture and tax efficiency
strategy_candidates.evidence: Kenneth demonstrates owning Tesla shares purchased years ago at low cost basis and supplementing with TSL covered call ETF to generate additional income while protecting against taxes and cost basis loss if called away—applicable to Brian's $50k Premium Desk targeting $1,250/mo premium

title: Covered Call ETFs: Pros and Cons #kennethsuna #investingforbeginners
